matches.match_rationale: The core pain is teachers spending 5+ hours weekly generating lesson plan prose — objectives, activities, assessments, instructions — all natural language output. Text generation directly replaces the blank-page drafting burden by generating structured lesson plan content from a topic, year level, and learning standard as inputs. For a multi-site operator, a shared prompt template ensures consistency across all 10-15 campuses while allowing teachers to refine rather than create from scratch.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A lesson plan generator where teachers input subject, year level, curriculum standard, and duration; the AI produces a full draft plan (objectives, activities, resources, assessment) in the school's house format, cutting drafting time from hours to minutes.

matches.match_rationale: Administrators currently manually collate submissions across campuses — this involves reading, condensing, and reporting on many individual lesson plans. Summarization can automatically distill a corpus of submitted plans into a coverage report, flagging gaps or redundancies, which removes the administrative collation bottleneck. For multi-site operators this is especially valuable: summarising what 10-15 campuses planned for a given unit reveals duplication instantly.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An automated collation tool that ingests submitted lesson plans from all campuses and produces a weekly summary report showing coverage, duplication hotspots, and curriculum alignment gaps — replacing the manual admin collation task.

matches.match_rationale: A major source of duplication is that teachers recreate plans from scratch because they cannot find existing plans from prior semesters or other campuses. Semantic search over a shared lesson plan repository lets a teacher query 'Year 8 fractions introduction with group activity' and surface highly relevant past plans — eliminating the blank-page problem without requiring exact keyword matches on titles or tags. This directly attacks the cross-campus duplication and semester-to-semester rework.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A lesson plan library with semantic search where teachers query by topic, year level, or pedagogy type and retrieve relevant existing plans from across all campuses and past semesters, reducing redundant creation to adaptation.

matches.match_rationale: RAG combines the library retrieval benefit with generation: a teacher can ask 'give me a lesson plan for Year 10 persuasive writing' and receive an AI-generated plan grounded in the school's own approved prior plans, curriculum documents, and pedagogical standards — not a generic internet output. This directly addresses both the blank-page burden and the lack of a centralized template system, while ensuring institutional consistency across campuses.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A grounded lesson plan assistant trained on the operator's own curriculum documents and historical plans; teachers describe their lesson need in natural language and receive a contextually appropriate draft citing relevant past plans and curriculum alignment.

matches.match_rationale: The administrative collation workflow — receiving plan submissions, checking format compliance, routing to curriculum coordinators, aggregating reports — is a multi-step document processing pipeline. A task automation agent can autonomously receive submitted plans, validate against a template schema, flag non-compliant submissions, and compile the cross-campus coverage report without human collation. This addresses the admin burden component of the pain point which is distinct from the teacher creation burden.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An automated lesson plan submission pipeline where a coordinator-facing agent receives plans from all campuses, checks format compliance, routes exceptions back to submitters, and publishes a weekly coverage dashboard — eliminating manual collation entirely.
